title: Lawfulness is a state of affairs, not a document
paras: First, the foundation: lawfulness is a state of affairs, not a document. A use doesn't become lawful because an authority issues a lawful development certificate, and doesn't become unlawful because a certificate can't be found. The certificate records a legal position that already exists. It confirms lawfulness — it doesn't confer it. Something is lawful, in planning terms, if it never needed permission, or if it once needed permission and the time for doing anything about it has run out. The planning system either never had a grip on it, or it has lost its grip. Permitted is a different animal — a positive act; someone said yes. A use can be perfectly lawful without anyone ever having approved it.

Most people — and a good many planners — reach for "will they give me permission?" when the question that decides the outcome is "is this already lawful?" They're not the same question, and they don't live in the same part of the system. One is about the future, and the merits of a proposal. The other is about the past — about facts and time, and about whether the planning system has any say left at all. A fortnight spent worrying about the wrong question is the commonest waste in this field.

There are three routes to lawfulness, and holding them apart prevents most of the confusion. First, the thing was never development at all — a change within a use class, or works that are not development. Second, the thing is development, but Parliament granted permission in advance, through permitted development rights. And third, the thing was a breach, but the enforcement period has expired, and immunity has hardened into lawfulness. Each route is proved differently. Identifying which one the case runs on is the first analytical step in every certificate matter.

This is one of the few corners of planning where the officer and the agent are asking precisely the same question, and need precisely the same answer. There's no discretion, no balance, no merits. A use either has gone on long enough, or it hasn't. A building either was substantially complete by a given date, or it wasn't. The question has a right answer, even when the answer is hard to prove — which is why a properly evidenced certificate application has a quality no planning application has. You're not trying to persuade anyone. You're trying to demonstrate a fact. And once granted, the certificate fixes a legal position that is very hard for anyone to go behind.

title: s191 or s192: which certificate
paras: Now — section 191 or section 192: which certificate? Section 191 certifies what already exists: an existing use, operational development already carried out, or a breach of condition that has become lawful. In other words, "I did this — is it now lawful?" Section 192 certifies what is proposed: "if I do this, will it be lawful?" One question resolves the choice: has it happened yet? The two prove different things. Section 191 proves a history, with all the evidence of dates and continuity that involves. Section 192 proves a proposition of law — that the proposed thing needs no permission. And the moment work has started, or a use has begun, you've crossed out of section 192 into section 191 territory. A proposed certificate can't tidy up something already under way.

Much section 192 work is use-class and materiality analysis. A change of use is development only if it's material — a matter of fact and degree, turning on the character of the use — and a change within a single use class is expressly not development at all, which is the engine of many proposed certificates. Sui generis uses sit outside the classes and fall to ordinary principles. Intensification is the subtle one. More of the same activity is generally not development — until the increase is great enough to change the character of the use, at which point it's a fresh material change. So a certificate for a use at one intensity doesn't licence the same use at a much greater one.

The other great section 192 territory is permitted development, and it's treacherous, because a P-D right is a conditional grant. Permission exists only if every limitation and condition of the class is met, and one exceeded dimension, or one missed criterion, means the national permission never covered the works at all. The prior approval trap is the sharpest edge. Keenan v Woking — reported at 2017, E-W-C-A Civ 438 — holds that a grant of prior approval is not determinative of whether the development is permitted development. It decides only the matters referred, not whether the proposal meets the class description. So a developer can build in reliance on prior approval — express, or deemed by non-determination — and still have built unlawfully. The certificate is the instrument that actually decides the question. Check Article 4 directions before asserting any P-D right. And remember that P-D allowances are often cumulative — a section 191 certificate for earlier works measures how much headroom remains.

The certificate protects exactly what it describes — no more, and no less. The description isn't the packaging; it is the product. Describe a use too narrowly, and part of what the client does is unprotected. Too widely, and the overclaim drags down the whole application, because the authority won't certify more than the evidence supports. "Commercial use" is useless. "Use of the land for the storage and distribution of building materials", with the yard and buildings identified on the plan — that can be certified and relied on. Where intensity matters, capture the scale as well as the character. The red line is doing legal work too. It should match the planning unit asserted, include the access, the parking and the yard that are genuinely part of the use, exclude what is not, and correspond exactly to the words. For section 192, describe the end state precisely, then build to it. A certificate for a five-person H-M-O doesn't protect a six-person fit-out.

title: Burden and standard of proof
paras: Next, the burden and standard of proof. The burden of proof sits entirely on the applicant — that's the principle from Gabbitas, reported at 1985, J-P-L 630 — and it inverts the habit of ordinary development management. The authority doesn't investigate for them, doesn't gather evidence against them, and needn't go to great lengths either way. It looks at what was filed, and asks whether it's enough. Confidence is not evidence, and the case is the applicant's to make. From the first client meeting, the question isn't "is this lawful?" It's "can I prove this is lawful — and with what?" The gap between those two questions is where hopeful applications quietly die.

The standard is the balance of probabilities — more likely than not, the ordinary civil standard. Not beyond reasonable doubt. Not certainty. And an authority that refuses because the applicant hasn't excluded every doubt has applied the wrong test. Here's the refinement that wins cases. Where the applicant's evidence is precise and unambiguous, and the authority has nothing of its own to contradict it, or to make it less than probable, there is no good reason to refuse — and evidence is not to be rejected merely because it's uncorroborated. That's Gabbitas again, and Ravensdale. "The declaration is uncorroborated and is therefore given little weight" — that's the wrong test, and a refusal vulnerable on appeal. The authority defeats an application not by demanding certainty, but by putting weight on the other side of the scale: the aerial photograph that doesn't fit, the enforcement visit interrupting the period, the declarations contradicting each other.

For the officer, determination is a different exercise from development management, and the habits must be left at the door. One question, asked coolly: is the matter lawful? Merits are irrelevant in both directions. It doesn't matter that the use is unneighbourly, or would never have been permitted — and it doesn't matter that the circumstances are sympathetic. A refusal for insufficient information is legitimate, and common. It isn't a finding that the development is unlawful — only that the applicant hasn't proved it lawful — and the applicant can cure it by applying again with better evidence. The officer needn't give the benefit of the doubt where the doubt arises from gaps in the applicant's own case.

The mechanics matter. The determination period is eight weeks, unless a longer period is agreed in writing. Silence past the deadline operates as a deemed refusal, opening the section 195 appeal — so letting the clock run out doesn't buy time. It surrenders control of the outcome to an inspector. The authority may grant in different terms from those applied for — certifying the narrower use the evidence actually establishes, rather than refusing outright. Good practice is to seek the applicant's agreement to the revised description, and a modified grant counts as a refusal in part, appealable as to the shortfall. And reasons must be given: clear, confined to lawfulness, and capable of defence — because a refusal that strays into planning merits is a refusal waiting to be overturned.

title: Evidence, continuity and gaps
paras: Now for evidence, continuity and gaps. The workhorse of an existing-use application is the statutory declaration, from someone who knows the history first hand — but a declaration is only as good as what's in it. "I have used the building as a workshop for many years" is nearly worthless. "I began using it in March 2009, five days a week, this is what I did, and these people saw me" — that's worth a great deal. Around the sworn statements sits the documentary evidence that tips a case from arguable to certain: council tax and rates records, utility bills consistent with the use, invoices, tenancy agreements, electoral roll entries. Contemporaneous documents have a quality recollection never has. They were created at the time, for an ordinary purpose, by someone with no stake in a planning argument not yet dreamed of. A dated invoice has no fading memory, and no interest in the outcome.

Aerial photography has quietly become one of the most decisive forms of evidence in the field, and it cuts both ways. It's the agent's best friend when it shows the structure standing in the claimed year — and the officer's best friend when it shows bare earth in the year the applicant swears the building was there. Anyone working in this area should know where to find historic imagery, because the other side certainly will. The officer's task is to test critically but fairly. Probe internal contradictions. Check declared dates against the council's records and the imagery. Weigh how close each witness actually was to the events — the neighbour of twenty years against the cousin who visited at Christmas. But never set the bar at certainty, and never reject an account merely for standing alone.

Most existing-use cases turn on a period, and most failures turn on a gap in it. The use or building must be shown across the whole relevant span, without a break that resets the clock. A short silence, with a clear pattern either side, may be bridged on the balance of probabilities. A long unexplained gap, or a positive sign the use stopped, is another matter. For the section 192 case, evidence works differently. There are no witness statements about the past — instead, a clear description and a legal argument: the plan, the measurements, the use class analysis, the P-D calculation. And the discipline of precision applies even more strongly, because a vague proposal cannot be assessed at all.

When a refusal comes, section 195 gives the appeal — against refusal, refusal in part, which includes a grant in modified terms, or non-determination. It's decided by an inspector, on the same single question, with no power to grant planning permission and no interest in merits. Beyond the inspector lies only the High Court, on a point of law. Choose the procedure to match the real dispute. Written representations where the fight is about what the law means on agreed facts. An inquiry where the fight is about whether the facts are as the applicant says — because a disputed account of twenty years' use needs testing on oath, and cross-examination is the appellant's friend as often as the authority's. And front-load the evidence. Material held back until appeal, that should have been filed at application, invites criticism and costs — and trying to win a disputed factual case on paper is fighting one-handed.

title: Time limits and when the clock starts
paras: Time limits — and when the clock starts. For breaches on or after the 25th of April 2024, a single ten-year rule applies in England. Section 115 of the Levelling-up and Regeneration Act 2023 in effect deleted "four" and substituted "ten". The former four-year rules — operational development, and change of use to a single dwellinghouse — survive only where the operative event predates the line: works substantially completed, or the change of use occurring, before the 25th of April 2024. The cliff edge is real. An extension substantially complete in June 2020 became lawful in June 2024, under the preserved four-year rule. The identical extension completed in June 2024 isn't lawful until June 2034. Which side of the line the operative event falls is now the first question on any existing-use matter — and it's worth six years either way. Wales is unaltered. Check the Welsh position separately.

For operational development, the clock starts at substantial completion — and Sage, in the House of Lords, requires a holistic approach. Not whether some part was finished, but whether the building the person set out to create was substantially complete as a whole. The unfinished house never starts its clock, and stays enforceable indefinitely. Counterintuitively, finishing the building is what starts the protection running. For a change of use, the clock starts when the material change occurs, and the use must then subsist continuously — for a dwelling, that means actual residential use as a separate dwelling, not a building merely fitted out and capable of it. Two regimes sit outside section 171B altogether. Unauthorised works to a listed building never become immune — no period, ever. That's section 38 of the Listed Buildings Act. And advertisements answer to their own code, with their own ten-year, continuity-dependent immunity under the 2007 Regulations — Hackney v JCDecaux, reported at 2022, E-W-H-C 2621, Admin.

Once the full period has run, the accrued right is hardier than instinct suggests. Panton and Farmer, reported at 1999, J-P-L 461: a lawful use is not lost by mere cessation. A dormant use is still an existing lawful use, capable of supporting a certificate. It's lost in only three ways: abandonment, the formation of a new planning unit, or a material change to a different use. Abandonment is judged objectively — that's Hughes, reported at 2000, 80 P and C-R 397, where thirty years of dereliction defeated the owner's professed intention to return — on the four Castell-y-Mynach factors: physical condition, length of non-use, any intervening use, and the owner's intentions. And Hartley, reported at 1970, 1 Q-B 413, supplies the underlying question: merely temporary cessation, or abandonment? A breach of condition behaves entirely differently — that's Nicholson, reported at 1998, 76 P and C-R 191. Its lawfulness lasts only while the breach continues. Comply for a spell and breach again, and a fresh breach starts a fresh clock. And the certificate confirming a lawful breach does not remove the condition — it remains on the permission.

Condition precedent is where the most damage is done. The Whitley principle: works carried out in breach of a condition are not a lawful commencement. So if the only works done within the time limit breached a condition precedent, the permission was never begun, and it has quietly lapsed — with everything built since unauthorised. Hart Aggregates makes the doctrine workable, with a two-stage filter. First, the condition must be genuinely framed so that commencement depends on it — "no development shall take place until". And second, it must go to the heart of the permission — something fundamental to what was permitted, not landscaping or facing bricks. Greyfort shows it biting. A condition requiring ground-floor levels to be approved before any work was a true condition precedent. The access works done in breach were no commencement, and a permission the owner believed alive for decades had expired. So discharge conditions precedent properly before starting — and keep the evidence.

title: Concealment: Welwyn Hatfield and after
paras: Finally, concealment — Welwyn Hatfield, and after. In Welwyn Hatfield, reported at 2011, U-K-S-C 15, a building permitted as a hay barn was fitted out as a three-bedroom house from the outset, and occupied undetected past the then four-year period. The Supreme Court refused the certificate on two grounds. The technical one: a building that was a dwelling from the first brick never underwent a change of use to a dwelling, so the dwelling rule simply did not apply — the precise wording of the rule decided the case. The decisive one: the statutory time limits are not to be read so as to let a person profit from their own deliberate deception — the Connor principle. Fidler, reported at 2011, E-W-C-A Civ 1159 — the house hidden behind straw bales — came out the same way. Deliberate physical concealment engaged the principle without any false statement on a form.

The limits matter as much as the principle, because an officer who cries deception at every evidential gap will come unstuck. The Connor principle bites on deliberate, positive deception integral to the scheme — a false shell, a lie to a direct question, a pattern of quiet steps each designed to keep the authority in the dark. It is not engaged by ordinary silence. There's no duty to march into the council offices and announce a breach, and the authority failing to notice something visible from the road is not concealment. Between the poles it's fact and degree — but the touchstone is dishonest conduct, not the mere passage of unnoticed time.

Parliament added a statutory route alongside the common law: the planning enforcement order, under sections 171BA to 171BC. It lets an authority apply to the magistrates, within six months of sufficient knowledge, where a breach has to any extent been deliberately concealed, for an order opening a year in which to enforce despite the expired limits. Jackson, reported at 2015, E-W-H-C 20, Admin, confirms the two routes coexist — the statutory scheme did not displace Connor. And the order's threshold, "to any extent", is wider, so a scheme that might survive a Connor argument can still be caught. A client who concealed has no safe harbour in time, by two separate routes — and the taint reaches the whole application.

Lawfulness and enforcement are the same coin, and the interface has hard edges. A certificate cannot be granted for a matter contravening an enforcement notice or breach of condition notice in force. That is an absolute statutory bar, at application and appeal alike — and pursuing a certificate appeal into it is costs-award territory. Once a notice is served, the lawfulness case is run instead through the enforcement appeal. Ground b — the matters did not occur. Ground c — they are not a breach; that's where the not-development and P-D arguments live. And ground d — it's too late to enforce; the immunity argument. All on the same burden, standard and evidence. Two tactical points complete the picture. A lawfulness ground available on an enforcement appeal and not run can be forfeited — the statutory code allows no second bite outside its own structure. And where a ground c or ground d appeal succeeds, section 177 gives the inspector a discretionary power to grant a certificate there and then — but only on an express request, made before the appeal is determined.
